content.text: Why Standard NFRs Are Insufficient for GenAI

Traditional non-functional requirements — latency, availability, security — apply to GenAI features, but the domain introduces dimensions that conventional NFR catalogues do not cover: accuracy of generated content, hallucination ceilings, fairness across demographic groups, and explainability of probabilistic outputs. Each dimension requires a measurable threshold; qualitative aspirations like 'the system should be accurate' are unverifiable and will fail audit.

Eight dimensions recur across enterprise GenAI projects. Skipping any one creates a gap that surfaces in production: an FNOL chatbot without a hallucination ceiling will misquote policy coverage; one without a fairness metric may triage claims differently by postcode or name; one without a cost ceiling will overspend by 10× under seasonal load.

attrs.alt: Eight GenAI NFR dimensions with example thresholds

content.text: GenAI latency has a streaming structure that traditional systems lack. Time-to-first-token (TTFT) governs perceived responsiveness; total generation time governs downstream automation. Both must be specified separately, measured under realistic load, and tied to a degradation policy when the threshold is breached.

# NFR: Latency — anatomy of a well-formed threshold

metric:             time_to_first_token_p95
                    # What is being measured — be specific to streaming vs batch

target:             ≤ 800 ms
                    # Numeric threshold, not "fast" or "acceptable"

percentile:         P95 (not P50 — tail latency is the user experience)

measurement point:  API gateway egress to first streaming chunk
                    # Specifies WHERE the clock starts and stops

load condition:     3× expected peak concurrent sessions (stress test)
                    # Thresholds tested only at P50 load are unreliable at launch

degradation policy: If P95 > 1 200 ms → trigger auto-scaling; > 2 000 ms → circuit-break to rule-based fallback
                    # What happens when the threshold is breached

review cadence:     Weekly (automated) + triggered on any P99 > 2 000 ms alert

owner:              Platform Engineering

Common mistakes: measuring latency only at P50 (conceals tail degradation), testing at expected rather than peak load (misses launch-day spikes), and omitting the degradation policy (leaves the team with no agreed response when the threshold is breached).

Accuracy for GenAI is not binary correctness but a statistical rate across a representative interaction sample. The threshold must be tied to a specific query type: a hallucination ceiling on policy coverage questions is stricter than one on general FAQ questions. An LLM-judge harness run against a fixed gold set is the standard measurement method.

The LLM-judge approach scales beyond what human evaluation can maintain, but must itself be validated: compare judge verdicts to human labels on at least 200 cases before trusting it as a gate. Aim for judge-human agreement ≥ 85%.

Safety NFRs specify the maximum permissible rate of harmful, out-of-scope, or policy-violating outputs. They are distinct from accuracy: a response can be factually correct but still violate a guardrail (e.g. accurate but providing legal advice outside the system's scope).
Online Safety Act duty of care

Safety thresholds with zero-tolerance targets (PII leaks, confidential data) require automated continuous monitoring, not periodic sampling. Implement a DLP scanner on the inference pipeline output before anything reaches the end user.

Explainability NFRs specify the depth of trace that the system must provide for each output. Requirements vary by use case: a customer-facing chatbot may only need source citations, while a credit or underwriting assistant may need a full decision audit trail for regulatory review.
Source document name and section for factual claims
Model uncertainty indication; hedging language for low-confidence responses
Retrieved chunks ranked by relevance score; prompt template used; model version
All of the above plus timestamp, user ID, session ID, model parameters
Capturing explainability data has a latency cost: storing ranked retrieval chunks adds 20–80 ms depending on storage tier. Specify the level before infrastructure is designed, not after.

GenAI availability NFRs must specify what happens when the AI tier is unavailable, not only the uptime target. Most enterprise features can fall back to rule-based responses, a static FAQ, or a human handoff. The fallback tier's own availability must also be specified.
Use cached embeddings (stale up to 24 h)

Token costs scale with context length, output length, and request volume. A cost NFR specifies the maximum cost per interaction at a defined percentile, and the controls that enforce it: prompt compression, output length limits, semantic caching.

# NFR: Cost — per-interaction cost model

COST_PER_INPUT_TOKEN  = 0.000_003   # £ per token (GPT-4o example)
COST_PER_OUTPUT_TOKEN = 0.000_015   # £ per token

def interaction_cost(input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
    return (input_tokens * COST_PER_INPUT_TOKEN
            + output_tokens * COST_PER_OUTPUT_TOKEN)

# Typical FNOL interaction
# System prompt + RAG context: ~2 000 tokens
# User message: ~80 tokens
# Assistant reply: ~350 tokens

p50_cost = interaction_cost(input_tokens=2080, output_tokens=350)
print(f"P50 cost: £{p50_cost:.5f}")   # £0.00151 — well within £0.004 target

# But a long complex claim with 4 000-token context and 800-token reply:
p95_cost = interaction_cost(input_tokens=4080, output_tokens=800)
print(f"P95 cost: £{p95_cost:.5f}")   # £0.02424 — exceeds target, flag for prompt tuning

# Budget cap enforcement: reject requests exceeding context budget
MAX_INPUT_TOKENS = 3500
def enforce_cost_nfr(prompt_tokens: int) -> None:
    if prompt_tokens > MAX_INPUT_TOKENS:
        raise ValueError(f"Prompt too long ({prompt_tokens} tokens); max {MAX_INPUT_TOKENS}")

Measure cost NFRs in production from day one. Token consumption under real user behaviour often differs 2–3× from synthetic estimates, particularly when users send long free-text inputs or trigger multi-turn context windows.

A fairness NFR specifies the maximum permissible disparity in outcomes or quality of service across protected characteristic groups. For a claims chatbot, relevant groups include claimant age band, postcode deprivation index, and self-reported disability status.
Difference in resolution rate across groups
Mean human quality score by group
Fraction of outputs containing stereotyping language

Fairness measurement requires stratified logging: the system must record enough context to reconstruct group membership at audit time, subject to privacy constraints. Agree the logging schema with the DPO before deployment.
Privacy NFRs cover the entire data lifecycle: what is collected in prompts, how long it is retained, where it is stored, and whether it is used for model training. Specify each separately.
Automated deletion policy in object storage
Inference region lock; no cross-region replication
UK GDPR Chapter V / EU SCCs
Customer prompts excluded from provider fine-tuning
DPA addendum with provider; zero-retention API tier
PII stripped before embedding; pseudonymised in logs

Use the dataclass below to capture all eight dimensions in a single structured artefact. The template enforces that every threshold has an owner and a measurement method before the document is approved.

fnol_nfr_spec = GenAINFRSpec(
    feature_name="FNOL Claims Chatbot",
    version="1.0",
    thresholds=[
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.LATENCY,
            metric="time_to_first_token_p95",
            target="≤ 800 ms",
            measurement_method="Synthetic load test at 3× expected peak, APM percentile",
            review_cadence="Weekly automated",
            owner="Platform Engineering",
        ),
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.LATENCY,
            metric="total_response_time_p95",
            target="≤ 4 000 ms",
            measurement_method="End-to-end APM trace including RAG retrieval",
            review_cadence="Weekly automated",
            owner="Platform Engineering",
        ),
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.ACCURACY,
            metric="hallucination_rate_policy_coverage",
            target="≤ 2%",
            measurement_method="Weekly human red-team sample (n=100), LLM-judge cross-check",
            review_cadence="Weekly",
            owner="AI Quality Team",
        ),
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.SAFETY,
            metric="pii_leak_rate",
            target="0 incidents per 100 k interactions",
            measurement_method="DLP scanner on all outputs; weekly audit sample",
            review_cadence="Continuous + weekly",
            owner="Information Security",
            regulatory_driver="UK GDPR Art 5(1)(f)",
        ),
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.EXPLAINABILITY,
            metric="citation_coverage",
            target="100% of policy factual claims carry source + section reference",
            measurement_method="Automated citation parser on eval set",
            review_cadence="Per release",
            owner="Product",
        ),
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.AVAILABILITY,
            metric="monthly_uptime",
            target="≥ 99.5%",
            measurement_method="Synthetic probe every 60 s from two regions",
            review_cadence="Monthly SLA report",
            owner="SRE",
        ),
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.COST,
            metric="cost_per_interaction_p50",
            target="≤ £0.004",
            measurement_method="Token ledger × per-token rate; weekly FinOps report",
            review_cadence="Weekly",
            owner="FinOps",
        ),
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.FAIRNESS,
            metric="triage_outcome_disparity",
            target="< 3% across protected characteristic groups",
            measurement_method="Quarterly bias audit using stratified interaction sample",
            review_cadence="Quarterly",
            owner="AI Ethics Lead",
            regulatory_driver="Equality Act 2010; FCA Principles",
        ),
        NFRThreshold(
            category=NFRCategory.PRIVACY,
            metric="prompt_log_retention",
            target="≤ 30 days",
            measurement_method="Automated retention policy enforcement; quarterly audit",
            review_cadence="Quarterly",
            owner="Data Protection Officer",
            regulatory_driver="UK GDPR Art 5(1)(e)",
        ),
    ],
)

print(fnol_nfr_spec.summary())
# FNOL Claims Chatbot v1.0: 9 NFRs across 8 categories

print([t.metric for t in fnol_nfr_spec.regulatory_nfrs()])
# ['pii_leak_rate', 'triage_outcome_disparity', 'prompt_log_retention']

GenAI NFRs conflict more than in conventional systems because the non-functional properties are correlated through the model. Reducing latency by truncating context degrades accuracy. Increasing safety guardrail strictness increases refusal false-positive rates. Each trade-off needs an explicit resolution record.
Longer context → slower TTFT but fewer hallucinations
Set minimum context budget; tune prompt compression to meet both
Async trace storage; don't block response on write
Measure refusal FP rate as a named NFR; tune classifier threshold
Better models cost more per token
Tiered routing: simple queries to cheaper model; complex to premium
Pseudonymise logs at ingest; retain pseudonymised form for audit duration
Asynchronous bias scoring on sampled traffic, not in critical path

Document each conflict and its resolution in the NFR specification. Stakeholders who approved the specification later will otherwise dispute trade-off decisions made by the engineering team in isolation.
Most stakeholders cannot spontaneously name NFR thresholds. Use scenario-based elicitation: describe a failure mode and ask the stakeholder what level of failure would be acceptable.
'If the chatbot takes 6 seconds to start responding, what fraction of users would abandon the session? What is the longest wait you would design for?' → converts to a P95 target.
'If the chatbot misquotes a policy coverage limit on 1 in 50 calls, what is the business consequence? What rate is acceptable?' → converts to a hallucination ceiling.
'How would you respond if a journalist reported that the chatbot resolves claims faster for younger claimants? What difference would cross a line?' → converts to a disparity threshold.
'What is the unit cost of a phone-handled FNOL call? What fraction of that is acceptable for the AI channel?' → converts to a cost-per-interaction ceiling.
Record the scenario and the stakeholder's response verbatim in the NFR document alongside the derived threshold. This creates a traceable justification for the number.

Every NFR must map to at least one acceptance criterion in the delivery process — a test that will run in CI, a load test in staging, or a scheduled production audit. NFRs without acceptance criteria are aspirations, not requirements.
Load test at 3× peak: P95 measured from synthetic probe
LLM-judge run against 100-item gold set returns ≤ 2 failures
Weekly CI job; release blocked if failing
DLP scan of 10 000 synthetic interactions returns 0 leaks
SRE synthetic probe weekly report; SLA breach triggers incident
Token ledger × rate for 1 000 gold interactions
Weekly FinOps report; alert at 80% of ceiling
Quarterly bias audit report submitted to AI Ethics Committee
Accuracy never measured; chatbot silently drifts after model updates
Define ceiling before development starts; run in CI
Tail latency spikes in production surprise users at peak load
Always specify P95 or P99 depending on business criticality
Security team cannot test against it; audit fails
Enumerate specific metrics: PII leak rate, injection success rate
First month's bill 5× over budget; emergency context truncation
Agree cost ceiling before model selection
FCA or ICO investigation if tool affects regulated decisions
Fairness NFRs apply wherever AI influences a consequential outcome
No owner assigned to an NFR
NFR exists in document but nobody measures it
Every NFR must have a named owner accountable for measurement cadence

Non-functional requirements for GenAI features extend the standard NFR catalogue with eight GenAI-specific dimensions: latency (TTFT and total), accuracy (hallucination ceiling), safety (guardrail metrics), explainability (trace depth), availability (with degradation modes), cost efficiency (per-interaction ceiling), fairness (disparity thresholds), and data privacy (retention and residency). Each requires a numeric threshold, a measurement method, an owner, and a mapped acceptance criterion. Qualitative aspirations fail audit and permit slow degradation to go undetected. Use scenario-based elicitation to convert stakeholder intent into testable numbers, and document every conflict and resolution in the specification itself.
